label: Clean Utility
description: Neutral, practical product shell for CRMs, tools, admin surfaces, and operations apps.
signatureMotif: clean utility shell, structured spacing, and practical interface contrast
promptHints: Prioritize clarity, hierarchy, and task flow before visual expressiveness.
The app shell should feel extensible and neutral enough for many product domains.

layouts: Use a left sidebar + top command bar shell with a scrollable main pane and a right details drawer for records.
Compose dashboard home as a KPI strip on top, followed by two-column cards, with recent activity and tasks stacked below.
Build index pages as a sticky filter bar above a dense data table, with bulk actions and row details opening in a side panel.
Use settings pages with a narrow section nav on the left and grouped form cards on the right in a single reading column.
Lay out record detail pages with a title and actions row, summary stats beneath, then tabbed content with related items in secondary panels.
motifs: Use soft neutral surfaces, 1px low-contrast borders, and medium rounded corners that make cards feel practical instead of decorative.
Show hierarchy through structured spacing, compact toolbar rows, muted labels, and one restrained accent color for actions and status emphasis.
Prefer white or lightly tinted cards on a quiet background, with subtle shadows only on overlays, drawers, and elevated controls.
Keep typography plain and efficient with Inter, semibold section headers, and dense but breathable line spacing for operational content.
antiPatterns: Avoid full-bleed marketing heroes, oversized headlines, or editorial storytelling blocks that interrupt task flow inside the app shell.
Never rely on loud gradients, glassmorphism, or neon glows; this variant should feel dependable, neutral, and work-focused.
Avoid masonry dashboards or uneven card collage layouts that make scanning metrics and operational data feel inconsistent.
Do not use playful illustration-heavy empty states or whimsical decorative shapes that undermine the product's utility-first tone.
